skill.name: caveman-stats
skill.description: Show real token usage and estimated savings for the current session. Reads directly from the Claude Code session log — no AI estimation. Triggers on /caveman-stats. Output is injected by the mode-tracker hook; the model itself does not compute the numbers.
skill.summary: Real session token receipts with no AI estimation. See exactly how many tokens and money caveman saved this session.
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: All 14 static analysis findings are false positives. The external_commands alerts flagged Markdown code formatting backticks as shell execution, and filesystem alerts flagged a legitimate relative documentation link. The weak crypto alert on SKILL.md line 3 was triggered by YAML folded block scalar syntax (description: >), not any cryptographic operation. The skill files contain no executable code, no network requests, no credential access, and no prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Backtick-enclosed command examples and file paths in README.md and SKILL.md were flagged as Ruby/shell backtick execution by the static analyzer. These are standard Markdown inline code formatting indicators, not shell command execution. Example: `/caveman-stats` in a Markdown code block is a user-facing command example, not a shell exec call.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Link Misidentified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The relative Markdown link `../../README.md` on line 30 was flagged as a path traversal sequence. This is a legitimate relative hyperlink pointing to the root README of the caveman repository, not a directory traversal attack.

low_findings.title: YAML Syntax Misidentified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The YAML folded block scalar indicator `>` on SKILL.md line 3 was flagged as a weak cryptographic algorithm. This is standard YAML syntax for multi-line string values, not a cryptographic operation of any kind.

content.user_title: Track token savings from caveman mode
content.value_statement: AI coding agents can produce long responses that cost tokens and money. This skill reads the Claude Code session log and shows exactly how many tokens caveman mode saved, with no AI estimation or guesswork.
actual_capabilities: Reads the current Claude Code session log from disk to report actual token usage
Compares input and output token counts against a non-caveman baseline estimate
Writes a lifetime savings suffix displayed on the Claude Code statusline badge
Triggers on the /caveman-stats slash command with instant output
Provides per-session turn count, input tokens, output tokens, and estimated savings percentage
limitations: Only works with Claude Code sessions that have a JSONL session log on disk
Baseline estimate may not perfectly reflect non-caveman output due to session variability
Does not track token usage across multiple sessions (single-session only)
Requires the caveman mode-tracker hook to be properly installed

use_cases.title: Measure caveman cost savings
use_cases.description: A developer wants to quantify how many tokens caveman mode saves per session to justify using it on their team.
use_cases.target_user: Developer or team lead evaluating caveman for their workflow

use_cases.title: Optimize session efficiency
use_cases.description: A power user runs /caveman-stats periodically to monitor their token consumption and adjust their prompting patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Frequent Claude Code user looking to reduce costs

use_cases.title: Report ROI to management
use_cases.description: An engineering manager collects token savings data from team member sessions to report cost reduction from adopting caveman mode.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering manager tracking AI tooling ROI

prompt_templates.title: Basic session stats
prompt_templates.scenario: New user checking token savings for the first time during a session

prompt_templates.title: Check savings mid-session
prompt_templates.prompt:
How many tokens have I saved so far?
/caveman-stats
prompt_templates.scenario: User working on a long task who wants to check cumulative savings

prompt_templates.title: Compare before and after
prompt_templates.prompt: Run /caveman-stats now, then I will switch modes and run it again to compare.
prompt_templates.scenario: User switching between caveman modes to compare token efficiency

prompt_templates.title: Report savings for documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the session stats so I can record the savings in our team documentation.
prompt_templates.scenario: User collecting data for a team report on AI tooling efficiency

output_examples.output:
Session: 47 turns
Input: 12,304 tokens
Output: 3,891 tokens (caveman)
Baseline: 11,247 tokens (estimated without caveman)
Saved: 7,356 tokens (about 65 percent)

output_examples.input: Check savings after a long debugging session.
output_examples.output:
Session: 123 turns
Input: 45,678 tokens
Output: 12,345 tokens (caveman)
Baseline: 38,000 tokens (estimated without caveman)
Saved: 25,655 tokens (about 67 percent)
Lifetime savings: 142,890 tokens across all sessions

best_practices: Run /caveman-stats at the end of a session for the most accurate full-session savings calculation
Use the lifetime savings displayed on the statusline badge to track cumulative impact over time
Compare stats across different caveman intensity levels to choose the most cost-effective mode for your task
anti_patterns: Do not run /caveman-stats mid-response because the hook interrupts and will return partial session data
Do not compare raw token counts between different session types without accounting for task complexity differences
Do not treat the baseline estimate as an exact measurement because it approximates non-caveman output from the same turns

faq.question: Where do the token numbers come from?
faq.answer: The numbers come directly from the Claude Code JSONL session log file on disk. The hook reads actual tokens recorded during your session, not AI estimates.

faq.question: How is the baseline calculated?
faq.answer: The baseline estimates how many output tokens a non-caveman response would have used for the same session turns. It is an approximation based on typical caveman compression ratios.

faq.question: Does this skill work with other AI coding tools?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for Claude Code and reads its specific session log format. It may not work with other tools unless they produce compatible session logs.

faq.question: Can I see lifetime savings across sessions?
faq.answer: Yes. Each time you run /caveman-stats, the hook writes a lifetime savings total to a suffix file. This value appears on the Claude Code statusline badge.

faq.question: Does this skill send data anywhere?
faq.answer: No. All data stays on your local machine. The skill reads a local session log file and writes a small statusline suffix file. No network requests are made.

faq.question: Why do I see no data when I run /caveman-stats?
faq.answer: The skill requires an active Claude Code session with a session log file on disk. If you just started a session, run a few prompts first so there is data to measure.
